content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

Date : 1/5/76

To : Telephone Memo

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
     Natalie West

Subject: JIM HEALY, LOBBYIST FOR THE SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED
         SCHOOL DISTRICT (12/31/75)

He is a registered lobbyist and, pursuant to Section 86107,
files statements including a "specific description of the
legislative or administrative action" that he has influenced
or attempted to influence. 86107(f). In addition, his
employer (the San Francisco Unified School District) files
reports including a "specific description of legislative or
administrative action which the person making the report
has attempted to influence." Section 86109(g).

Question: How specifically does the employer report
          describe the activities of other employees
          of the School District, who are not lobbyists,
          but who contact administrative officials with
          regard to pending regulations, etc.? §86109(g).

Answer:   Apparently we have no clear guidelines on this
          topic. I advised him that even if the employer
          does not have to report compensation paid to the
          employee as "payments to influence" because the
          employee spends less than 10% of his/her time
          communicating with administrative officials
          (2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18620(a)(4)), the
          employer should still list the actions which
          the employee attempted to influence, i.e.,
          regulations pertaining to textbook acquisition,
          etc. I told him not to report every conversa-
          tion, and not to report communications which
          are informational and don't constitute attempt-
          ing to influence administrative action.

Okay?


NEW:PVP

sections.question:
How specifically does the employer report describe the activities of other employees of the School District, who are not lobbyists, but who contact administrative officials with regard to pending regulations, etc.? §86109(g).

Answer: Apparently we have no clear guidelines on this topic. I advised him that even if the employer does not have to report compensation paid to the employee as "payments to influence" because the employee spends less than 10% of his/her time communicating with administrative officials (2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18620(a)(4)), the employer should still list the actions which the employee attempted to influence, i.e., regulations pertaining to textbook acquisition, etc. I told him not to report every conversation, and not to report communications which are informational and don't constitute attempting to influence administrative action.

Okay?
